clues.clue: This scope exam screens for colorectal cancer and is recommended starting around age 45.
An at-home stool test checking for hidden blood is abbreviated FIT, the F meaning this.
Low-dose CT scans are used to screen long-term heavy smokers for this cancer.
A blood test for a specific antigen, abbreviated PSA, screens for cancer of this gland.
Skin exams watch these pigmented spots for changes that may signal melanoma.
Striking the colon or rectum, this cancer is among the most common in men over 50.
The leading cause of cancer death in men, usually smoking-related, attacks these organs.
The most common non-skin cancer in men arises in this walnut-sized gland.
This deadliest skin cancer arises from the body's pigment-producing cells.
Often called 'silent,' this organ's cancer has low survival and ties to smoking and diabetes.
Unexplained loss of this, without trying, can be a red flag for cancer.
A lasting change in these habits, or blood in the stool, may signal colorectal cancer.
A persistent one of these, especially in a smoker, warrants checking for lung cancer.
In the ABCDE melanoma rule, the 'A' stands for this lack of matching halves.
Trouble swallowing, with food seeming to stick, can point to cancer of this food tube.
This single habit causes roughly a third of cancer deaths and is the top preventable cause.
Overexposure to these invisible sun rays drives most skin cancers.
A vaccine against this virus, abbreviated HPV, prevents cervical and some throat cancers.
Diets heavy in processed and red ones of these are linked to colorectal cancer.
The bacterium H. pylori raises the risk of cancer in this acid-filled digestive organ.
A non-cancerous tumor that does not invade or spread is called this.
A cancerous tumor that invades tissue and spreads is termed this.
The spread of cancer from its origin to distant body sites is this process.
A physician who specializes in diagnosing and treating cancer is this '-ologist.'
The system rating how far a cancer has progressed, often from I to IV, is this.

final.clue: This treatment aims high-energy beams at tumors to kill cancer cells, distinct from drug-based chemotherapy.
